topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email sat on the screen, stark white against the muted grey of my desk. *Regretfully, your application for the Archimedes Scholarship has not been selected.* My fingers hovered over the mouse, then slowly retreated, resting flat on the cool laminate. A strange, hollow echo seemed to fill the small room. I stared at the blinking cursor, unable to form a coherent thought. My stomach tightened into a knot, a cold, heavy pressure that made it hard to draw a full breath. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding unnaturally loud in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, looking out at the bustling street below, but the motion of the cars felt distant, as if I were observing a movie from behind thick glass.

[story 2]

"No." The word came out as a dry whisper, barely audible above the hum of the refrigerator. I reread the rejection letter for the tenth time, the carefully chosen words blurring into meaningless shapes. My hands began to tremble, a fine, almost imperceptible vibration running up my arms. I gripped the edge of the wooden table until my knuckles turned white. Everything felt tilted, as if the floor beneath my feet had suddenly shifted its alignment. I stood up abruptly, needing movement, needing to disrupt the stillness that was consuming me. I paced three steps to the kitchen counter and stopped, leaning against the cool granite. My gaze drifted around the familiar space, seeing the chipped mug, the stack of untouched textbooks—nothing registered clearly.

[story 3]

The phone call had ended minutes ago, leaving a ringing silence in its wake. I kept staring at the phone, the screen reflecting back a face that looked strangely unfamiliar. A slow, creeping sense of unreality washed over me, like wading through thick, lukewarm water. I tried to focus on the task at hand—reorganizing my notes—but the ink seemed to swim before my eyes. Every sentence I tried to write felt clumsy, heavy, as if the words themselves had weight. I picked up a pen, the plastic feeling slick and alien in my palm. I tapped the tip against the paper repeatedly, a frantic, repetitive action intended to anchor me, but the effort only seemed to drain the remaining energy from my limbs.
